coredata.dc:title: The hierarchical layered microsphere of BiOIxBr1-x solid solution decorated with N-doped CQDs with enhanced visible light photocatalytic oxidation pollutants

coredata.dc:description: The hierarchical layered microspheres of BiOIxBr1-x solid solution decorated with N-CQDs were rapidly synthesized by one-step in-situ co-precipitation. Nitrogen doping caused carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CQDs, These groups combined with Bi atoms in bismuth-derived precursors to provide in situ growth site for BiOIxBr1-x, thus achieved strong binding effect between BiOIxBr1-x and N-CQDs, which was beneficial to charge transfer. Meanwhile, BiOIxBr1-x hierarchical layered microspheres were assembled by nanosheets with a thickness of about 23 nm, which could generate multiple reflection of light, and thus enhanced the light-harvesting. Under visible light irradiation, N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 presented the remarkable photocatalytic activity when the loading amount of N-CQDs was 1.25 wt%. The removal rate of phenol and tetracycline hydrochloride was 98.7% within 3 h and 96.6% after 5 min, respectively. The reason of improvement activity of N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x was due to the multiple scattering of light between hierarchical layered BiOxBr1-x as well as the internal electric field from N-CQD to BiOI0.25Br0.75.

head.ce:title: The hierarchical layered microsphere of BiOIxBr1-x solid solution decorated with N-doped CQDs with enhanced visible light photocatalytic oxidation pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hierarchical layered microspheres of BiOIxBr1-x solid solution decorated with N-CQDs were rapidly synthesized by one-step in-situ co-precipitation. Nitrogen doping caused carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CQDs, These groups combined with Bi atoms in bismuth-derived precursors to provide in situ growth site for BiOIxBr1-x, thus achieved strong binding effect between BiOIxBr1-x and N-CQDs, which was beneficial to charge transfer. Meanwhile, BiOIxBr1-x hierarchical layered microspheres were assembled by nanosheets with a thickness of about 23 nm, which could generate multiple reflection of light, and thus enhanced the light-harvesting. Under visible light irradiation, N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 presented the remarkable photocatalytic activity when the loading amount of N-CQDs was 1.25 wt%. The removal rate of phenol and tetracycline hydrochloride was 98.7% within 3 h and 96.6% after 5 min, respectively. The reason of improvement activity of N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x was due to the multiple scattering of light between hierarchical layered BiOxBr1-x as well as the internal electric field from N-CQD to BiOI0.25Br0.75.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic wastewater with high toxicity, stabilization and difficult to biodegradation, seriously threatened the survival of human beings and marine organisms [1–5]. Therefore, many treatment methods, such as, membrane filtration, adsorption, sedimentation, coagulation/flocculation, anaerobic/aerobic were commonly used to dispose phenolic wastewater. However, these methods only transferred the organic pollutants from the wastewater phase to another phase, and could not completely remove phenolic compounds. In addition, these methods produced secondary pollution easily. The photocatalytic technology has attracted widespread attention due to high efficiency and environmentally-friendly for pollutants treatment [6–9].

Bismuth based photocatalysts have been extensive studied because of their excellent light response and chemical stability, especially their unique electronic band structure and controllable morphology [10]. At present, many kinds of bismuth based photocatalysts have been reported, such as BiVO4, Bi2WO6, Bi2MoO6, BiPO4, Bi2S3, BixTiOy, (BiO)2CO3, Bi2O3 and BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I). Among them, the unique layered structure of BiOX was composed of [Bi2O2]2+ plates and double X-plates alternately along the Z axis. The opened layered structure could form built-in electric field, which was conducive to promoting the separation and mobility of photogenerated electron hole pairs [11]. In addition, the VB of BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) was composed of O2p orbitals and Xnp (n = 3, 4, 5) orbitals. Meanwhile, CB was mainly dominated by Bi6p orbitals that had similar layered crystal structure. So, it was benefit to the formation of their solid solutions. The solid solution could form uniform energy levels owing to the hybridization on the atomic scale, and thus prohibited carrier recombination. Therefore, the band structure of photocatalyst could be precisely regulated by changing the type of halogen atom and the stoichiometric ratio of Bi, O and X [12,13]. Furthermore, it was reported that the photocatalytic activities of BiOClxI1-x [14–16], BiOClxBr1-x [17–19], BiOIxBr1-x [20–24], BiOClxBryIz [25] were better than that of the corresponding BiOX. However, these studies were focused on the photocatalytic degradation of dyes, such as RhB, Mo and MB. Nevertheless, there were few studies on the removal of stable colorless phenol pollutants. In addition, the improvement of photocatalytic performance was depended on the doping of noble metals, for example, Au-BiOCl1-xBrx [26], Ag3PO4-BiOCl1-xBrx [27], Pd-BiOBr [28], which could not meet the requirements of low cost, high efficiency and stability [1,29].Therefore, to explore a low-cost modification method to improve the separation efficiency of carrier of solid solution BiOIxBr1-x was significant.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown great application potential in the field of photocatalysis due to low cost, non-toxicity, good water solubility and simple synthesis route [30,31]. CQDs were a type of carbon nanomaterial, which were composed of discrete quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with the size of below 10 nm. CQDs had the traditional down conversion photoluminescence, but also had the unique up conversion photoluminescence. Therefore, CQDs could convert visible light into ultraviolet or shorter wavelength light, and make use of sunlight potentially. In addition, CQDs had excellent properties of electrons transfer and storage, which could be used as co-catalyst to form a spatial separation center of photogenerated carriers [32–34]. However, the application was hindered due to the poor transfer ability of internal electron. Recently, the research showed that nitrogen doping CQDs could effectively induce charge delocalization, reduce work function, promote the photoluminescence performance, and improve the photocatalytic performance [35–38]. Because the combination of CQDs and semiconductors was usually prepared by mechanical mixing (in ethanol or water) and drying, the composite was bonded by van der Waals force, and then the bonding interface was consolidated by annealing or hydrothermal method for long time [32]. Therefore, to modify BiOIxBr1-x solid solution by using low-cost N-CQDs not only improved the photocatalytic activity of BiOIxBr1-x, but also strengthened the combination capacity of CQDs and BiOIxBr1-x. Moreover, up to date, the research on N-CQDs modified BiOIxBr1-x solid solution had not been reported.

Thus, in this paper, BiOIxBr1-x solid solution decorated with N-CQDs was synthesized by one-step in-situ co-precipitation. The crystal phase, morphology, optical absorption and photoelectrochemical properties of photocatalyst were systematically studied by lattice constant calculation and series characterization. Moreover, the energy band structures, density of states, work function and charge density differences were calculated depend on Density Function Theory (DFT). Under visible light, the removal effect phenol and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) over N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x were studied. The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x was proposed by using active species capture, ESR and O2 − quantitative experiments. The in-situ precipitation strategy of N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x could provide reference for the rapid synthesis of CQDs modified semiconductors.

All used chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, P. R. China, which were reagent grade and used without further purification. BiOIxBr1-x solid solution was prepared by a one-step coprecipitation at room temperature (Scheme 1 ). 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 30 mL glycol and treated ultrasonic into a transparent solution. Afterward, 30 mL solution containing 4 mmol KX solution (X = I, Br; the molar ratio of I and Br was 1:0, 0.75:0.25, 0.5:0.5, 0.25:0.75, 0:1, respectively) was added dropwise under magnetic stirring. Subsequently, the obtained mixture was stirred for 2 h continually. Then, the solid was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

N-CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal method (Scheme 2 ) [38]. 3 g citric acid and 1 g urea were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and treated with ultrasonic for 2 h. Then, the mixture was transferred to 50 mL stainless steel reactor, which was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Afterward, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged to remove large particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and dialyzed in deionized water (MWCO 2000) for 24 h to remove small impurity. Finally, the supernatant was dried to obtain N-CQDs powders at 60 °C for 12 h. CQDs were obtained in the same process except adding urea.

The preparation of N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x was shown in Scheme 2. 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 30 mL glycol and treated ultrasonic into a transparent solution. Then, a certain amount of 1 mg/mL N-CQDs solution (3, 6, 9, 15, 21 mL) was added and stirred vigorously for 10 min. Afterward, KX solution was added dropwise (X = I, Br; Imol : Brmol = 0.25 : 0.75). Subsequently, the obtained mixture was stirred for 2 h continually. Then, the solid was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. y wt% (y = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.25, 1.75) N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 samples was obtained, and named y NCQDs-BIB (y = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.25, 1.75).

The crystal structure of the as-prepared samples was determined by 7000 X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu, Japan), Cu Kα, Ni filter, λ = 0.15418 nm, 2° min−1 of scanning speed, 40 kV, 30 mA. The morphology of the as-prepared samples was measured by JSM-6700 scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan). The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) of the as-prepared samples was measured by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The surface functional groups of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by IRAFFINITY-1S Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Shimadzu, Japan). The element composition and chemical state of the as-prepared samples were determined by PHI-5400 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PE, U.S.A.). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–Vis DRS) of the as-prepared samples was determined by UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), BaSO4 as reference standard, 200–800 nm of scanning range. The Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area of the as-prepared samples was determined by ASAP 2010 (micrometrics, U.S.A.). The pore size distribution was measured by Barrett Joyner Halenda (BJH) model. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The photochemical properties of the as-prepared samples were tested by CH1660D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China) with three electrode cell. ITO glass coated with the as-prepared samples was used as working electrode, Pt electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution was used as electrolyte, and the light source was 300 W xenon lamp (λ ≥ 400 nm). The TOC removal rate was determined by F3000 TOC analyzer (Analytik, Germany).

The photocatalytic properties of the samples were determined in a 500 mL quartz reactor. Using 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300/300UV, Beijing Perfectlight, China) with 400 nm cut-off as visible light source, 0.2 g catalyst was dispersed in 200 mL 10 mg/L phenol solution (or 20 mg/L TC-HCl solution) at room temperature. The solution was stirred for 30 min under dark to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, turn on the lamp, in a certain interval, the residual concentration of phenol was determined by 4-aminoantipyrine and HPLC. The residual concentration of TC-HCl was determined at 357 nm by UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species capture and O2 − quantitative determination
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiments of capturing the active species, P-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and EDTA-2Na were used to capture the O2 −, OH and h+ [39,40]. Under the irritation of visible light, DMPO (5, 5′- dimethyl-1-pyrroline-n-oxide) as spin trap, the free radical signal was determined by JES-FA300 electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer. The quantitative determination of O2 − was carried out by the nitrotetrazolium chloride (NBT) conversion. In a typical experiment, 100 mL 2.5 × 10−5 mol/L NBT solution, 0.1 g catalyst, and the residual concentration of NBT was measured at 259 nm [41].

Fig. 1 (a) showed the XRD spectrum of as-prepared BiOIxBr1-x (x = 1, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25, 0). When × was 1 or 0, the characteristic diffraction peaks of BiOI and BiOBr was corresponded to the tetragonal BiOI (JCPDS no. 10–0445) and tetragonal BiOBr (JCPDS no. 73–2061), respectively. No other peaks were detected, which indicated that the BiOX (X = I, Br) had high purity. Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks with strong and sharp features showed that the crystallinity of the as-prepared samples was well. With the increase of Br content (x decrease from 1 to 0), the diffraction peak of 2θ = 31.65° was coincided with (110) crystal plane shifted to the large angle direction. The phenomenon could be observed in the enlarged XRD spectrum with 2θ = 26° − 35° (Fig. 1 b). It indicated that the BiOIxBr1-x solid solution was formed between BiOI and BiOBr [21] . Meanwhile, no diffraction peaks of pure BiOI and BiOBr were observed in the XRD patterns of BiOI0.75Br0.25, BiOI0.5Br0.5 and BiOI0.25Br0.75, which had similar tetragonal structures. These results further indicated that the BiOIxBr1-x solid solution was formed [16,42,43]. It was due to that the ion radius Br− (rBr- = 0.196 nm) was smaller than I− (rI- = 0.220 nm). Therefore, Br− was easier to replace or occupy the position of I−. Furthermore, the difference between the rBr- and rI- was only 10.9% (<10% − 15%), which was in favor of the formation of solid solution [20]. From Fig. 1 (c), the experimental value of lattice parameters of the homogeneous BiOIxBr1-x solid solution was accorded with the vergard's law, which conformed to t(x) = x tBiOI + (1-x) tBiOBr, where t was the lattice parameter (a, b, c), x is the content of I atom [17,23]. With the increase of × (I content), the experimental value of lattice parameter almost increased linearly. In addition, the increase rate of c parameter (slope was 1.0375) was higher than that of a or b (slope was 0.0665). It was that the atoms in [Bi2O2]2+ layer of BiOI was connected by the strong covalent bond, while the double iodine layer was combined by weak van der Waals force. Therefore, the change of c crystal axis caused by Br substitution was large, and the change of a and b crystal axes was small [23,25,44]. In Fig. 1 (b), the shift level of diffraction angle of (102) crystal plane was greater than that of (110) crystal plane, and the decreasing value of (102) crystal plane spacing (Δd (102)BiOI→BiOBr = 0.020 nm) was greater than that of (110) crystal plane spacing (Δd (110)BiOI→BiOBr = 0.005 nm). The scheme of the crystal structure of the solid solution was shown in Fig. 1 (d), and the experimental values of lattice parameter were shown in Table 1 . Fig. S1 (c) showed the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQD and N-CQDs aqueous solution. In the wavelength range of 220 nm – 400 nm, N-CQDs presented higher absorption intensity, and there were two obvious absorption peaks at 240 nm and 330 nm, which corresponded to π – π* of aromatic sp2 conjugate fields and C = O double bond, respectively [38]. In addition, the photoluminescence properties of N-CQDs were measured (Fig. 1e). In the excitation wavelength range of 500–800 nm, the emission peak was obviously observed in the range of 360–560 nm. The results showed that N-CQDs had unique up conversion photoluminescence properties, which could convert long wavelength light into short wavelength light, and resulted in an improved photocatalytic activity. Fig. 1 (f) showed the XRD patterns of BiOI0.25Br0.75 and y NCQDs-BIB. The diffraction pattern of NCQDs-BIB was consistent with BiOI0.25Br0.75, approximately, indicating that the introduction of N-CQDs could not change the crystal structure of BiOI0.25Br0.75. Furthermore, the characteristic diffraction peak of N-CQDs was not obvious. It was due to the dispersion of N-CQDs in the surface of BiOX was extensive, and the size of N-CQDs was small. Meanwhile, comparison with BiOX (inorganic semiconductor), the crystallinity of N-CQDs was poor, and the diffraction pattern was weak.

The morphology of the as-prepared samples was shown in Fig. 2 . According to Fig. 2(a), (b) and (c), BiOBr, BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB presented the regular microspheres morphology with an average diameter of 1.5 μm. Moreover, these microspheres were assembled by nanosheets with a thickness of about 23 nm. Generally, the light-harvesting can be enhanced by the hierarchical layer structure which could generate multiple reflection of light [42]. From of Fig. 2(d), BiOI0.25Br0.75 showed the microsphere with a diameter of 0.5–1.5 μ m, which was consistent with the SEM results. In the HR-TEM of BiOI0.25Br0.75 (Fig. 2e), the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.827 nm and 0.284 nm was coincided with (001) crystal planes of the tetragonal BiOxBr1-x (between d BiOBr(001) ~ dBiOI (001) = 0.807 nm ~ 0.915 nm) and (102) crystal planes (between d BiOBr(102) ~ dBiOI (102) = 0.281 nm ~ 0.301 nm)[16], respectively. From the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) (insert of Fig. 2e), the obvious diffraction ring distribution and bright diffraction spots showed that BiOI0.25Br0.75 had polycrystalline characteristics and good crystallinity. In addition, the corresponding diffraction spots of (102) crystal planes and (004) crystal planes were calibrated. These results were further confirmed that the formation of solid solution between BiOI and BiOBr [45]. According to Fig. 2 f, g, N-CQDs with an average diameter of about 7 nm attached to BiOI0.25Br0.75 nanosheet, formed an interface between N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.7. As shown in Fig. 2h, i, the lattice spacing of 0.827 nm, 0.284 nm and 0.332 nm was corresponded to (001), (102) crystal planes of BiOI0.25Br0.75 and (002) crystal planes of graphite phase, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical composition and functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectrums of BiOI0.25Br0.75, N-CQDs and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB were shown in Fig. 3 a. As shown in Fig. 3a, the peaks of 3850 cm−1, 3748 cm−1, 3440 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 were corresponded to water molecules or hydroxyl groups [46]. The peak of 1075 cm−1 was accorded with the symmetric stretching vibration of Bi-X (X = Br, I) bond of BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB. The peak at 510 cm−1 belonged to the symmetric stretching vibration of typical Bi-O bond [41,47,48]. In addition, the peaks located at 3214 cm−1 (C-OH and N–H), 1562 cm−1 (CO), 1432 cm−1 (–NH), 1284 cm−1 (C-N) and 1136 cm−1 (-C-NH-C-) was observed obviously in the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs, indicating that a variety of polar groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, amino) existed on the surface of N-CQDs [38]. The nitrogen doping could act as binding sites to coordinate with Bi ions [49], in order to improve the stability of NCQDs-BIB. However, these peaks were obviously weakened or disappeared in the FT-IR spectrum of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB. The functional groups, such as carboxyl group, amino group of N-CQDs combined with Bi atom to form situ growth site. Therefore, the situ precipitation nucleations, microcrystalline growth, anisotropic growth, self-assembly were occurred on the surface of N-CQDs with high surface energy. Moreover, the size of BiOI0.25Br0.7 was larger than N-CQDs, which resulted to partially coating of N-CQDs in BiOI0.25Br0.7. From EDS of Fig. S2, 1.25NCQDs-BIB was composed of Bi, O, I, Br, C and N elements, and the distribution of elements was basically consistent with the morphology area, which further indicated that NCQDs was successfully introduced into the solid solution BiOI0.25Br0.75.

According to XPS full spectrum (Fig. S1a), the detected elements were consistent with the corresponding composition of BiOI0.25Br0.75, N-CQDs and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f (Fig. 3b), the binding energies of 159.2 eV and 164.5 eV corresponded to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which implied Bi3+ existed in BiOX [17]. In high resolution XPS of Br 3d(Fig. 3c) and I 3d (Fig. 3d), the peaks at 68.2 eV and 69.3 eV were attributed to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, the peaks of 619.1 eV and 630.6 eV were accorded with I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 respectively, which indicated that Br− and I− coexisted in BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB [21]. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. S1b), there were two characteristic peaks at 530.1 eV and 531.3 eV in BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB, respectively, which corresponded to the Bi-O bond of [Bi2O2]2+ layer in the BiOI0.25Br0.75 and the water molecule or ·OH group adsorbed on the surface [41]. In the high resolution XPS of C1s (Fig. 3e), there were three peaks of 284.6 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.4 eV in NCQDs-BIB, which belonged to CC, CO and C-N bonds, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS of N1s (Fig. 3f), The binding energy of 399.4 eV and 400.9 eV were in accord with C-N-C and N-(C)3 bonds [36,38], respectively. Therefore, there were many chemical functional groups (such as carboxyl, amino) on the surface of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB, and N-CQDs were attached in the surface of BiOI0.25Br0.75 by certain chemical adsorption [50].

To examine the interaction between N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75 surface, the interfacial cohesive energy was calculated as follows,

where E(N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75), E(N-CQDs), E(BiOI0.25Br0.75) represented the total energies. The interfacial cohesive energy of N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 was −2.07 eV, which indicated there was a strong binding effect between N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75 to form a stable composite. The stably interlayer distances in N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 was about 3.00 Å, not only smaller than layer distance of graphene (4.34 Å) but also smaller than interlayer distances in graphene/MoS2 (3.37–3.40 Å) interfaces [51,52]. Thus, it was not a physical adsorption, but likely some chemical adsorption in interlayer. It supported the XPS and FT-IR characterization result that functional groups, such as carboxyl group, amino group of N-CQDs combined with Bi atom of BiOI0.25Br0.75.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and BET specific surface area
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis DRS of BiOIxBr1-x (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1) solid solution were shown in Fig. 4 a. The absorption edge of BiOxBr1-x (x = 0) was 452 nm. With the increase of x value, the absorption edge red-shifted, and the maximum absorption edge shifted to 696 nm, which was the absorption edge of BiOI (x = 1). The band gap of photocatalyst was calculated as shown in Fig. 4b. Eg of BiOIxBr1-x (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1) was estimated to be 2.74 eV, 2.00 eV, 1.92 eV, 1.85 eV, 1.78 eV, respectively. The results showed that BiOIxBr1-x solid solution was formed between BiOBr and BiOI [22]. Fig. 4c showed the UV–Vis DRS of y NCQDs-BIB. With the increase of y value, the absorption intensity increased. According to Fig. 4d, The N2 adsorption desorption isotherm of 1.25NCQDs-BIB was of type II [22]. BET specific surface area of 1.25NCQDs-BIB was 20.97 m2/g, which could provide more active sites and facilitate mass transfer. From the insert of Fig. 4d, the pore size distributed in the range of 30–90 nm, indicating that there were meso-pores and macro-pores in 1.25NCQDs-BIB, which could be considered to be the formation with smaller nanosheet.

According to DFT calculation (Calculation methods and Models was shown in S. M.), the electronic band structures of the BiOIxBr1-x were 1.93 eV for BiOI, 2.58 eV for BiOBr and 2.09 eV for BiOI0.25Br0.75, illustrated in Fig. 5 a, b, c, which were in good agreement with the experimental values (~1.78 eV, ~2.74 eV, and ~ 2.00 eV, respectively). Based on the above comparison of the band gaps of BiOIxBr1-x, it was considered that the calculation method was reasonable and reliable. Fig. 5d, e, f presented the total and partial densities of states of BiOBr, BiOI and BiOI0.25Br0.75, respectively. Consequently, the VBM was consisted of the O 2p orbitals, Br 4p orbitals and I 5p orbitals, and the CBM was composed of Bi 6p orbitals, which were consistent with the literature [16,53].

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared sample for phenol was shown in Fig. 6 a. The photocatalytic degradation rate of pure BiOI and BiOBr was only 49.7% and 12.3%. However, the photocatalytic performance of solid solution BiOIxBr1-x was significantly improved. While BiOI0.25Br0.75 showed the best activity, and the photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol was 66.9%. It was worth noting that the introduction of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic phenol degradation rate of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB was as high as 98.7% under 3 h of visible light irradiation. The photodegradation properties of the photocatalysts in this work and other published similar photocatalysts were compared in Table 2 . Under approximate same condition, the photocatalytic activity of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB was almost equal to or better than the reported activity of bismuth oxyhalide modified with noble metal (Pd, Au, Ag, Pb). In addition, the photocatalytic degradation of phenol conformed to the first-order kinetics (Fig. 6b), the apparent rate constants Kaap of BiOBr, BiOI, BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB were 0.04 h−1, 0.23 h−1, 0.35 h−1 and 1.46 h−1, respectively (Fig. 6c). Thus, the rate constants of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB was 6.3 times of BiOI, 36.5 times of BiOBr and and 4.2 times of BiOI0.25Br0.75. In order to further determine the reliability of the photocatalytic activity of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB, the residual concentration of phenol was determined by 4-aminoantipyrine method and HPLC (Fig. 6d, e). As shown in Fig. 6d, e, the evolution spectrum of phenol concentration showed an identical downward trend, the photocatalytic degradation rates were 98.7% and 98.6% respectively, with a difference of only 0.1%. As shown in Fig. 6f, The TOC removal rate of phenol of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB was about 88.4% within 3 h of visible light irradiation, which indicated NCQDs-BIB had remarkable mineralization ability. The stability analysis of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB was shown in Fig. S3d, after three cycles, the degradation rate decreased from 98.7% to 98.1%. Consequently, 1.25 NCQDs-BIB had excellent photocatalytic stability.

The transient photocurrent response (PC) spectrum of BiOI, BiOBr, BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25NCQDs-BIB were shown in Fig. 7 a. The photocurrent density of all of samples was nearly unchanged in the five on/off cycles of visible light, indicating that all of samples had high photo-response stability. The photocurrent density of pure BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 was 0.23μA/cm2, and 0.56μA/cm2, 2.43μA/cm2, respectively. While photocurrent density of 1.25NCQDs-BIB was obviously increased to 3.17μA/cm2, which was 13.8 times of BiOI, 5.7 times of BiOBr, and 1.3 times of BiOI0.25Br0.75. It indicated that the separation efficiency of carriers was remarkable heightened. Fig. 7b was the Nyquist spectrum of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The Nyquist curve radius of 1.25NCQDs-BIB was the smallest, which indicated that the migration resistance of carriers was the smallest and the transfer of interface charge was the fastest. These results were consistent with those of photocatalytic degradation of phenol, which revealed the reason of enhanced activity. The Mott-Schottky (M−S) curve of BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 was shown in Fig. 7c. The estimated values of flat band potential (Vfb) of BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 were 0.66 eV, −0.32 eV and 0.05 eV vs Ag/AgCl, respectively. Meanwhile, the slope of the linear part of the M−S curve was>0, indicating that BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 were n-type semiconductors. Generally, for n-type semiconductors, Vfb was equal to the Fermi level (Ef), and the conduction band position was more negative (0.1–0.3) than Ef. Furthermore, according to UV–Vis DRS, Eg (vs NHE, pH = 6.8) of BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 was calculated to be 1.78 eV, 2.74 eV, and 2.00 eV, respectively. Therefore, According to ECB(NHE, pH = 6.8) = Efb(Ag, AgCl) + 0.199–0.2 [41], ECB (vs NHE) of BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 was 0.66 eV, −0.32 eV and 0.05 eV (vs NHE), respectively. So, EVB of BiOI, BiOBr and BiOI0.25Br0.75 was + 2.44 eV, +2.42 eV, +2.05 eV (vs NHE), which was consistence with the reported value [18].

The photocatalytic degradation of phenol over 1.25 NCQDs-BIB with the addition of BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na was shown in Fig. 8 a. As shown in Fig. 8a, the degradation rate was 98.68% without adding capture agent. After adding BQ (O2 −) and EDTA-2Na (h +), the photocatalytic degradation rate was 8.95% and 79.93%, respectively, the degradation effect was obviously inhibited. However, the degradation rate was unchanged with the adding of IPA (·OH). Therefore, O2 − and h + were the main active species of photocatalytic degradation of phenol. ESR spectrums of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB were shown in Fig. 8b, c. As shown in Fig. 8b and c, the intensity of ESR signal of O2 − and ·OH increased with the prolonging of illumination time, indicating that·O2 − and ·OH were produced in the photocatalysis process. EVB (2.05 eV) of BiOI0.25Br0.75 was positive than ·OH/OH− (+1.99 eV), as a result, h + could oxidize H2O (OH−) to produce ·OH. As shown in Fig. 8d, The NBT conversion rate of BiOI, BiOBr, BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB were 15.1%, 12.7%, 27.8% and 78.6%, respectively [41,55]. As shown in Fig. 8e, the absorbance intensity at 259 nm decreased significantly. Within 50 min of visible light irradiation, the O2 − yields of BiOI, BiOBr, BiOI0.25Br0.75 and 1.25 NCQDs-BIB were 0.83 × 10−5, 0.67 × 10−5, 0.91 × 10−5 and 3.28 × 10−5 mol/L, respectively. Furthermore, the O2 − production rate of 1.25 NCQDs-BIB was 6.56 × 10−7 mol/(L·min).

To further gain a thoroughly understanding of the interface charge transfer between CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75, the work function was estimated to obtain the electrostatic potential of Fermi levels, as shown in Fig. 9 a, b (Calculation methods and Models was shown in S. M.). The work functions of (102) surface of BiOI0.25Br0.75 and N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 were 5.13 eV and 4.53 eV, respectively. After the introduction of N-CQDs to BiOI0.25Br0.75, the work functions of N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 were decreased. Therefore, during closely contact of N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75, electrons could flow from N-CQDs to BiOI0.25Br0.75. This result could be directly confirmed by 3D charge density differences of N-CQD/BiOI0.25Br0.75. Here, the charge density difference and Δρ was obtained as follows

were the charge densities of N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75, isolated N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 9c, after equilibrium, the charge density differences mainly occurred at interface of N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75. The charge densities at interface were redistributed by forming electron accumulation (yellow iso-surfaces) and electron depletion (blue iso-surfaces) in the space with respect to the isolated N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75, respectively. According to bader charge analysis, the number of transferred electrons from N-CQDs to BiOI0.25Br0.75 was 0.08 eV. Therefore, BiOI0.25Br0.75 gathered more negative charge and the N-CQDs gathered more positive charge. As a result, an internal electric field from N-CQDs to BiOI0.25Br0.75 was formed, which was favorable for the separation and transfer of interface carriers.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of 1.25NCQDs-BIB was proposed (Fig. 10 ). BiOI0.25Br0.75 had a suitable band gap (2.00 eV) and layered structure. Under the excitation of light of 400 nm < λ < 585 nm, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs were formed. At the same time, due to the performance of PL up-conversion of N-CQDs, the light of λ1 > 585 nm was converted into the visible light with λ2 < 585 nm. Hence, more photogenerated electron hole pairs were produced. Moreover, N-CQDs had good electron enrichment ability. Therefore, the CB electrons of BiOI0.25Br0.75 were rapidly transferred to N-CQD. As a result, the photogenerated electron hole pairs could be effectively separate. A large amount of O2 − was produced after the capture of dissolved oxygen in the reaction system. On the other hand, due to the positive VB potential (+2.05 eV) of BiOI0.25Br0.75, h + with strong oxidation capacity directly participated in the photocatalytic degradation. The visible light ability was enhanced by the synergism of the multiple scattering of light between hierarchical layered BiOxBr1-x and PL up-conversion N-CQDs. Meanwhile, as the center of electron enrichment, N-CQDs improved the performance of separation and transfer of photogenerated electron hole pairs. Furthermore, after formation of N-CQD/BiOI0.25Br0.75, an internal electric field from N-CQDs to BiOI0.25Br0.75 was formed, it was favorable for spatial separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers.

N-CQDs/BiOIxBr1-x hybrid microspheres were synthesized by one-step in-situ co-precipitation. The hybrid microspheres were assembled by nanosheets with a thickness of about 23 nm, the cross layer structure could enhance the light-harvesting. The formation of solid solution was revealed by analysis of the experimental lattice parameter of BiOIxBr1-x, which was in accordance with the vergard's law. When the load content of N-CQDs was 1.25 wt%, the photocatalytic degradation activity of N-CQDs/BiOI0.25Br0.75 was optimal, and the removal rate of phenol and TC-HCl was 98.7% and 96.6% within 3 h and 5 min, respectively. The visible light ability was enhanced by the synergism of the multiple lights scattering of hierarchical layered BiOxBr1-x and PL up-conversion N-CQDs and the internal electric field between N-CQDs and BiOI0.25Br0.75. The research could provide the reference to enhance the photocatalytic activity by the synergistic effect of solid solution and quantum dots, and explore the method of the modification BiOIxBr1-x with noble metals in the photocatalytic field.
